title: Heat pump installation: what the process involves
subtitle: A decision guide for homes considering an air source heat pump
excerpt: An air source heat pump replaces a gas or oil boiler and provides both heating and hot water. The installation involves a site survey, possibly a cylinder upgrade, radiator assessment, and commissioning. Understanding the process helps set realistic expectations.
sourceNotes: Boiler Upgrade Scheme (BUS) eligibility guidance from DESNZ (2024). MCS MCS 020 standard for heat pump installation. PAS 2035 framework for whole-house retrofit. Energy Saving Trust air source heat pump guidance.

content.text: delivers 2.5 to 3.5 units of heat for every unit of electricity consumed, measured as

. At UK electricity and gas prices (electricity approximately 24p/kWh, gas approximately 6p/kWh), a heat pump needs an SCOP above 4 to break even against a gas boiler on running costs. Most UK installations achieve SCOP of 2.5 to 3.5. The financial case for a heat pump is currently environmental rather than financial in most UK homes. The upfront cost may be partially offset by the

-certified installer will carry out a heat loss calculation to size the heat pump correctly. This involves measuring the building fabric, windows, and loft insulation and calculating the peak heat demand. A 3-bedroom semi typically has a peak heat demand of 5 to 9 kW depending on insulation level. The heat pump output at design condition (usually -3°C for UK) must meet this demand.
The surveyor will also assess radiator sizes relative to the target
. Radiators sized for 70°C gas boiler operation deliver significantly less heat at 45 to 50°C. Undersized radiators mean the heat pump must raise flow temperature to compensate, reducing its
. Radiator upgrades are often part of the installation package.

Air source heat pumps are permitted development in England for most properties (since April 2022), provided the outdoor unit does not face a highway, is not within 1 m of the property boundary, and is not in a listed building or conservation area. Scotland and Wales have separate rules. The installer will confirm PD eligibility as part of the survey.
Outdoor unit: fixed to a plinth or wall bracket on an outside wall, ideally facing prevailing wind for airflow. Refrigerant pipework runs through the wall to the indoor unit.
Indoor unit or hydrobox: holds the heat exchanger, expansion valve, controls, and on some models a backup electric heating element. About the size of a boiler. Usually fitted in the same spot.

Hot water cylinder: most combi-boiler homes do not have a cylinder. Heat pump systems require a separate hot water cylinder of 150 to 250 litres. This is the largest physical change in a typical installation.

Electrical supply: heat pumps require a dedicated circuit from the consumer unit. A 3-5 kW unit draws 15-25 amps at peak; total daily consumption is typically 2000-4000 kWh per year for a 3-bedroom house.

attrs.body: The Boiler Upgrade Scheme pays a significant grant toward the installation of an air source heat pump. The grant is paid directly to the MCS-certified installer and deducted from the quoted price. The installation must be by an MCS-certified company. As of 2024, the scheme is open until March 2028. Heat pumps installed under BUS must also meet minimum efficiency standards set by MCS 020.
